That's why, for example, it's harder to sell something to ice cream shop owners than it is to steakhouse owners because their transaction size is different, right? However, and I did not make this point, I don't think up until now, I mentioned price elasticity a little, but to say it even with greater clarity, the further we are over into selling opportunity, the less this matters. We liberate ourselves from these formulaic relationships the more we are actually in the opportunity business because what you're doing now doesn't matter, right? It's what's got to happen. So consequently, again, if you look at pure opportunity marketers, so if you look at Coscontent Turner, so this is now late 60s, early 70s, this is a $5,000 sale, right? Probably the average income of the buyer was below $15,000 a year, and many of them got to go borrow the money to get it. When we were selling a $30,000 program to chiropractors in the late 1970s, we're taking at least 100% of a year's income, maybe more, right? And at least 50% of them got to get a bank loan or a finance company loan in order to make the purchase, right? So these ratios that we all know as info marketers and we deal with and we're governed, we're somewhat restricted by, and in some cases we leverage, these ratios, if they don't entirely go away, they at least can become damned elastic when we start to sell opportunity. So you do want to keep that in mind. And there's a big difference between their income and their ability to buy, right? That's gotten closer together again right at the moment because people's credit card limits are being, cards that didn't have limits are getting limits put on them, and it's not so easy to roll down to household finance or Benny and get along, that whole thing is, but still, people's income is not necessarily logically connected to their ability to buy because they can go get money or will go get money to make the purchase. In the, during the Carter years, if you go back and look at all my stuff, you never saw an order form that didn't have a way for people to split the purchase amount up and tell you put this much on this credit card, this much on this credit card, this much on this credit card. And I almost started doing it again last year and probably should have. So with that said now, go ahead. But that's really an important point for everybody because we all have these logical connections in our head. And in fact, it's how we sell training, coaching, seminars, et cetera, but opportunity starts to take it apart. So one of the thoughts I had was to have, instead of $3,400, is to either have a sample starter kit. Okay, if $3,400, you're still skeptical and not sure, do we put a $300, $297 product? As long as you don't show it to them when you're trying to make the $3,400. Well, that's what I was like, will it, will it, first off, will it? Yes, it will. Okay, so. Yeah, look, people will trade down. So like I showed, in your manuals, I showed you the example yesterday on downsells, you know, our go by the inch downsell. There's very few circumstances where you, where you ever show it. Until after you've tried everything. Until after you've tried to make the big, sure, because they'll trade down. So that's the same reason. See if you, if you get them so they want to scratch their itch. So that's the same reason why a cafeteria speaker from the front of the room, so you sell from the front of the room, you get this. So Zig, Brian, cafeteria sells, right? You can go back there and buy one cassette album. Charlie Tremendous Jones, God bless him, you could go back and buy one book. Well, all you got to do is shrinkwrap all that shit up and let them only buy package A, package B, package C. You immediately increase the guy's revenue because he's go scratch his itch for the lowest amount of money he can get away with scratching his itch for. And the very story of if you're not sure, if you're not ready, if you can't afford, try this small thing, encourages it. So no, you want it, you want it after the fact. And is there a percentage of price that makes sense? Like, I mean, just, I mean, if you tested any, it doesn't matter half, 75% of it. There's no big broad answer to that. And it doesn't, and you may want multiples. I mean, you want, you may want to keep dropping. So you know, you can't sell it at 3,400 if you can sell some piece of it for 9.97. And then all the ones that you absolutely can't sell to 9.97, maybe you want to sell 2.97. And remember, you want to loop back up. We tested a dollar, just FYI, we tested like $1. We've got tons of refunds. Well, of course. Yeah, at some point you're buying business you don't want. Right, that's what I'm worried if there's a percentage. See, that's the, and a couple people said yesterday they're using it. See, the flaw in the, the flaw in the, oh, Jeff talked about it. So the flaw in the, and again, I'm not saying not to do it. But the flaw in the, this is a $25,000 do-hickey. You pay us $5,000 and you pay us the other $20,000 after you start making money. The flaw in that is that since most of them are never going to make money, but they would continue to spend money with you, they now go hide from you because they owe you 20 Gs that they're never going to pay you. And so now they don't want to be around you at all. Whereas if that wasn't hanging over their head, they'd buy another $5,000 thing and another $5,000 thing and another $5,000 thing. So you buy business that actually wasn't good business to buy in the first, so yeah, you, you can't go too low. Okay. I mean, look, some people, A, shouldn't, you know, you don't want them. And some people also never go buy. I mean, there was a reason they didn't buy that has nothing to do with price. Okay. Okay. Yeah, we're good to do you both. So order doesn't matter. Go ahead. So your conversation with Robin earlier got me thinking about the disadvantages that I may have in my market because I'm a woman selling into a very traditional male arena. And I've thought about it a lot post, you know, prior to this. So can you just talk a little bit about that? Like should, you know, you've, you know, where I have my picture on, you know, do I have my picture in my, you know, but it's something I've thought about for a while. Well, I mean, there's always four choices, right? We can hide entirely, right? That imposes severe restrictions on us. A quasi version of that is corporate institutional voice rather than personal voice, but that imposes severe restrictions as well. But it is one of the four options. So we can hide entirely. The second option is, is we can ignore it. Right? It is what it is. We don't address it. We don't call attention to it. We just sell like hell what we have to sell. Right? Three, we can make a big damn deal out of it. Right? And we can address it. Right? Four, we can try and help it. So we immediately help it. For example, if we pair you with a elder statesman from your industry who is very palatable to them, we help it even further if we do Justice League of America where there's five superheroes, not one. And now we got an old guy. We got a young guy. We got, you know, now we help it even further. So those are our four options. We, James used to joke about it, but he did it. So we had in the Tollison stuff, the thing you saw the letter for. Turner really did it differently. So James' deal was he figured out that to some segment of the audience he was inherently untrustworthy because he's hokey and country. So he created a Justice League of personality types and appearances to reassure everybody. So we always joke. We had this guy's name was Jack O'Brien and Jack had been a Sinclair oil executive his whole life and he looked like vice president of Sinclair oil. He couldn't help himself. That's what he looked like. You couldn't fix him even if you wanted to fix him. He had just the right haircut and gray hair. He would cast him maybe as vice president of the United States in a movie.
